w:r.w:t: Implementation alignment document reflecting the recipe-layer constraint for the recipe dataset and the current confirmed stack.

This revision incorporates the newly locked provider decisions from the latest project discussion: Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant is now the conversational LLM and the recipe-adaptation model, Gemini 2.5 Flash is the stable multimodal vision model, and the recipe layer is explicitly locked to retrieval-first grounded summary/light adaptation rather than default free-form recipe generation.

NutriAI is an AI-powered nutrition assistant with two primary user journeys: packaged-food understanding through barcode lookup and non-barcode food understanding through image-based recognition. A separate recipe layer supports recipe suggestion and conversational recipe assistance after ingredient or dish recognition. The conversational layer is now locked to Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant, while the no-barcode vision flow is locked to Google Gemini 2.5 Flash.

The recipe dataset discussed in the latest update does not replace any previously selected source. It is an internal recipe corpus used after recognition, not a general lookup database. Its current implementation mode is retrieval-first: retrieve grounded recipe candidates first, then use Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant only to summarize, explain, and lightly adapt those retrieved recipes.
Camera, barcode scan, UI, local session state, API calls
Auth, routing, normalization, caching, orchestration, persistence
Barcode lookup, product facts, ingredients, allergens
nutrition.xlsx + Egyptian Food.csv + FoodData Central fallback
Primary generic food nutrients from nutrition.xlsx, Egyptian dish grounding from Egyptian Food.csv, and FoodData Central Foundation/SR Legacy fallback for unmatched foods
Meal image understanding for foods with no barcode; structured candidate extraction
Recipe retrieval, candidate ranking, grounding, missing-ingredient logic, recipe discussion context
Grounded explanations, comparison summaries, follow-up Q&A
Grounded recipe summary, explanation, and light adaptation of retrieved recipe candidates
• Flutter must call FastAPI endpoints only.
• FastAPI may call Open Food Facts, Google Gemini 2.5 Flash, nutrition.xlsx, Egyptian Food.csv, FoodData Central fallback datasets, the recipe corpus, and Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant.
• API keys, vendor response parsing, nutrition grounding logic, retrieval logic, and recipe adaptation logic stay on the backend.
• The recipe dataset is not called directly by the mobile app and is not exposed as a standalone user-facing data source.
• The recipe dataset does not participate in barcode lookup or packaged-food lookup, and Gemini responses must be normalized into a stable backend-defined JSON contract before mobile delivery.

User scans barcode in Flutter -> Flutter sends barcode to FastAPI -> backend queries Open Food Facts -> backend normalizes nutrition, ingredients, and allergens -> normalized response returns to Flutter.

User captures image in Flutter -> Flutter uploads image to FastAPI -> backend calls Gemini 2.5 Flash -> backend extracts dish candidates, ingredients, and confidence into a structured JSON contract -> backend maps recognized dish or ingredients through the nutrition source hierarchy: nutrition.xlsx first, Egyptian Food.csv for Egyptian foods, then FoodData Central fallback -> backend returns nutrition estimate plus confidence.
User selects two products or meals -> backend computes a structured comparison -> Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant converts that comparison into concise grounded text.

User submits pantry items or recognized ingredients -> recipe layer retrieves recipe candidates from the recipe corpus -> backend computes match score, missing ingredients, and warnings -> Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant summarizes and lightly adapts the grounded candidates -> response returns to Flutter.

User asks follow-up questions about a suggested recipe -> backend retrieves the candidate recipe rows and structured match context -> Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant answers using grounded recipe context instead of free-form recipe invention.
5. Recipe-layer role of the recipe dataset
The recipe dataset is now explicitly constrained to recipe-facing use cases only.
• Recipe retrieval from recognized ingredients or pantry items.
• Candidate ranking by ingredient overlap, exclusions, and missing-ingredient count.
• Grounding for recipe suggestions so the system can point to specific matched recipes.
• Context for recipe discussion, such as explaining why a recipe was suggested, what is missing, and how it could be adapted.
• Seed material for controlled recipe summary and light adaptation, such as simplifying steps, clarifying order, or suggesting bounded substitutions without inventing a substantially new recipe.
The first implementation mode is locked to retrieval-first grounded assistance. Default free-form recipe generation is out of scope unless added in a later revision.
product_id, name, brand, nutrition, ingredients, allergens, source
matched dish, serving assumptions, nutrients, confidence, source
pantry items or recognized ingredients, dietary filters
recipes, match score, missing ingredients, warnings, source
recipe id or candidate context, user question
answer, grounded recipe references, safety flags
answer, source references, safety flags, latency
• All nutrition outputs should pass through one internal schema before leaving the backend.
• Barcode and vision flows remain separate upstream, but the mobile client should receive a consistent shape for nutrients, ingredients, allergens, confidence, source metadata, and safety notes.
• Recipe outputs should use a recipe-specific normalized shape that includes recipe id, title, matched ingredients, missing ingredients, exclusions, and grounding metadata.

• Conversational responses about recipes must be grounded in retrieved recipe rows and structured match context; Gemini vision output should also be normalized into a backend-owned JSON schema before any downstream use.

nutrition.xlsx is the primary nutrient lookup source; Egyptian Food.csv still requires schema, provenance, unit, and licensing validation before production trust, and FoodData Central remains the backend fallback.
Recipe dataset requires normalization of cleaned ingredients, duplicate handling, and basic dietary/allergen tagging before production use.
Keep provider adapters around Gemini 2.5 Flash and Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant so vendors can be swapped later.
Freeze mobile-facing response contracts early; route all upstream changes through backend normalization.
Recipe discussion responses must cite retrieved recipe candidates internally and avoid free-form recipe invention beyond bounded summary/adaptation.
Keep MVP recipe support bounded to grounded suggestions, missing-ingredient logic, and light adaptation.
• Mobile framework remains locked to Flutter.
• Backend framework remains locked to FastAPI running on Python.
• Flutter does not call Gemini, Open Food Facts, the recipe dataset, or Groq directly.
• The backend owns security boundaries, caching, vendor integration, and response normalization.

• Open Food Facts remains the packaged-food source; nutrition.xlsx is the primary food nutrient dataset, Egyptian Food.csv is the localized Egyptian-food dataset, and FoodData Central Foundation/SR Legacy remain fallback sources.
• Google Gemini 2.5 Flash is the locked multimodal vision model for the no-barcode flow; FastAPI must normalize its output into a stable JSON contract.
• Groq llama-3.1-8b-instant is the locked conversational model and the locked recipe summary/light-adaptation model.
• The recipe dataset is a recipe-layer-only corpus. It is not part of lookup, barcode retrieval, or authoritative nutrition grounding.

• Recipe assistance is retrieval-first and limited to grounded summary, explanation, and light adaptation of retrieved candidates; default free-form recipe generation is not part of the current baseline.
Recipe agent/provider and the final split between retrieval, adaptation, and generation.
Versioning and extension rules for future Gemini model or schema changes.
Formal validation result for Egyptian Food.csv and the production matching rules between nutrition.xlsx, Egyptian Food.csv, and FoodData Central fallback.
Recipe corpus preprocessing standard: canonical ingredient vocabulary, deduplication, dietary tags, and exclusion rules.
